ui.bank_sub: Very simple to advanced Python DSA practice.
ui.bank_tip: Select a question, copy starter code, solve in the editor, then compare with hints.
ui.editor_tip: Copy the starter code, paste it into the editor, and test with the examples.

questions.title: Print a Square Star Pattern
questions.prompt:
Write a Python function square_pattern(n) that prints an n by n square of stars.

Each row should contain n stars separated by spaces.
hints: Use an outer loop for rows.
Use an inner loop or string multiplication for columns.
Print one row at a time.
questions.starter_code:
def square_pattern(n):
    # write your code here
    pass

square_pattern(3)

questions.title: Print a Triangle Number Pattern
questions.prompt:
Write a Python function triangle_numbers(n) that prints a triangle of numbers.

For n = 4, first row has 1, second row has 1 2, third row has 1 2 3, and so on.
hints: The row number controls how many numbers to print.
Use range(1, row + 1).
Build a row string and print it.
questions.starter_code:
def triangle_numbers(n):
    # write your code here
    pass

triangle_numbers(4)

questions.title: Create Prefix Sums
questions.prompt: Write a Python function prefix_sums(arr) that returns a new list where each element stores the sum from the start of the list up to that index.
hints: Keep a running total.
Add each number to the running total.
Append the running total to the result list.
questions.starter_code:
def prefix_sums(arr):
    # write your code here
    pass

print(prefix_sums([2, 4, 1, 3]))
questions.solution_code:
def prefix_sums(arr):
    result = []
    running = 0

    for num in arr:
        running += num
        result.append(running)

    return result

print(prefix_sums([2, 4, 1, 3]))

questions.title: Count Positive and Negative Numbers
questions.prompt:
Write a Python function count_pos_neg(arr) that returns a dictionary with two keys: 'positive' and 'negative'.

Zero should not be counted as positive or negative.
hints: Use two counters.
If num > 0, increase positive.
If num < 0, increase negative.
questions.starter_code:
def count_pos_neg(arr):
    # write your code here
    pass

print(count_pos_neg([3, -1, 0, -5, 8]))

questions.title: Range Sum Using Prefix Sum
questions.prompt:
Write a Python function range_sum(arr, left, right) that returns the sum of elements from index left to index right.

Use a prefix sum list. Both left and right are included.
hints: First build prefix sums.
If left is 0, answer is prefix[right].
Otherwise answer is prefix[right] - prefix[left - 1].
questions.starter_code:
def range_sum(arr, left, right):
    # write your code here
    pass

print(range_sum([2, 4, 1, 3, 6], 1, 3))

questions.title: Maximum Sum of 3 Consecutive Elements
questions.prompt:

Assume the list has at least 3 elements.
hints: Start by checking the first 3 elements.
Move one position at a time.
For every group of 3, update the best sum.

def max_sum_three(arr):
    # write your code here
    pass

print(max_sum_three([2, 1, 5, 1, 3, 2]))

Check only j after i to avoid duplicate pairs.
A sum is even when (a + b) % 2 == 0.

Slide the window by adding the next element and removing the first old element.
Keep the best sum.

questions.title: Longest Word in a Sentence
questions.prompt:
Write a Python function longest_word(sentence) that returns the longest word in the sentence.

If there is a tie, return the first longest word.
hints: Use split() to get words.
Track the best word found so far.
Update only when the new word is longer.
questions.starter_code:

    # write your code here

questions.title: Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters
questions.prompt:
Write a Python function longest_unique_substring(s) that returns the length of the longest substring without repeating characters.

Use the sliding window idea.
hints: Keep a left pointer.
Use a set for characters in the current window.
When a repeated character appears, move left until it becomes safe.
questions.starter_code:
def longest_unique_substring(s):
    # write your code here
    pass

print(longest_unique_substring('abcabcbb'))
questions.solution_code:
def longest_unique_substring(s):
    seen = set()
    left = 0
    best = 0

    for right in range(len(s)):
        while s[right] in seen:
            seen.remove(s[left])
            left += 1

        seen.add(s[right])
        current_length = right - left + 1
        if current_length > best:
            best = current_length

    return best

print(longest_unique_substring('abcabcbb'))
